speaker: Rob Moore
words: The following tape was made with Florence Lenny Ingle in her home on Bear Ridge. She grew up in Little Bear Ridge and knew almost all of the people in the Troy and Kendrick area in the early days. Her father was a farmer and she married a farmer, King Ingle, and many of her memories and stories are on this tape of the days of horse farming, thrashing crews, fruit growing, early schools, and cattle wrestlers.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When did you come to the county?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: In 1884. We came, my train, from Wrigley, California, that area anyway, really was our county seat at that time.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: They called it an immigrant train. They were mostly immigrants. It wasn't very fancy, you know, and our seats were not really upholstered. They had good seats in there, but they had stoves too, you know. I guess with the immigrant trains they always had the stoves for heating in those days. They used wood. Anyway, I was only two years old. One thing I remembered on that train was seeing these strange people. They were always interesting to me. I would go from one seat to another, I suppose. I would be up in their faces, you know. One lady, just across the aisle from where we were sitting, she handed me out an orange. I didn't know it was an orange. I had heard of lemons, but I didn't know about oranges. I was only two years old. I took it over to my folks. My mother took it, and, oh, it's an orange. But that was the first time I had ever seen an orange. I don't know whether it was going to California at that time or not. That was back in 1884. I thought very much that they were growing. They might have been trying some orchards out, some trees out, but the oranges came later. Of course, they grew lemons. That's the reason I thought it was a lemon. It was yellow. I had never seen an orange.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did your father leave the area then?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Well, this father had grown up in Missouri. He was interested in stock back there.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Here in this Feather River in California, it was rich farmland. I forget how many acres he had there. It was a large area. The farming is quite heavy. He had to do it all with horses in those days. He did raise good crops. But out of seven crops, he lost three.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: The river came down from Oroville down through there, down through Gridley. We were about eight miles from Gridley. That was all in a nice valley through there. Well, Father's Bank was just in the right place for the water. Her belly levees would break up there. The miners would throw up levees and let the water down on our farm land. So Father lost three crops out of seven. That was a little discouraging to him. Our crops were heavy, though, and he replaced very good grain. He had very heavy yields on the grain.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He grew up in the stock country and he wanted to get into the stock business. He was great to read. Idaho had been advertised even down there as a new stock country. He had a brother -in -law. My mother's brother had moved into that section over there, which is where we moved. They had been living there three years before we went there. So they were quite well established. That was one reason why we went there, because this brother of hers, we knew him very well. She thought a great deal of him, keeping a civil war veteran.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I should go on. Here's my story. We found it all right there on Little Bear Ridge. We settled. We came to Moscow and stayed overnight there. We perhaps stayed several days there. Then we moved out to our homestead on Little Bear Ridge.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was the name of this brother -in -law?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: My brother -in -law.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He would be your uncle?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He was my uncle. His name was Porter. Liddell Porter. He had come from Tennessee, where my mother had lived in Tennessee. They had lived in Tennessee. They had come west and they were living down near Dayton. They had moved up here before we came from California. He was one of the earliest settlers on Little Bear Ridge. I have a list of names. I have the data on when they moved here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would you be interested in that?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: We can get that later. Okay then. I have them in there. I'll have to put them up up there in my little jar.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your father take out a homestead when he came here?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yes, he took a homestead. The farmland that we bought. His father had sold his property down there. He had some money. We didn't come here as helpers without anything. He bought livestock that he could get along with on the farm. He had a couple of cows.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Two horses at first was all he could get. He finally picked up more. So he began finding the land there. It was good farmland. He set out one of the first things he did. He did that before he came. He came up here before the family. The summer before. He set out at an archery. He found the land where he was going to settle in his homestead. He set out at an archery. He set out all kinds of trees. He set up pear trees, apple trees, peach trees, and prune trees. He had quite a bit of a leave. He must have had about ten acres of prunes. These Ikea that he put on. They were just planting them. People were putting up dryers. People that had come before that had their prunes up. They were putting up dryers and drying their prunes. It was quite a sale for dried prunes. In those days there was lots of demand for things like that over in the mines.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: They would come to our local stores and fill up at our local stores for their supplies or the mines. So there was a good sale for prunes. The father thought he could dry prunes. He built a nice little dryer on the place. And it was a long, fine job. He came in for supper one evening and looked out. The dryer was all the way so he lost all of our dried prunes except to some that they bought into the house. That was kind of a blow to us. He didn't go to work. The dryer had been back to Missouri and he had gotten money from his estate back there. I think his father had died, I believe. They allowed him his parts to be scared in the estate. He put most of that money that he had gotten into that prune car so he felt pretty badly about it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did it crash?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: We never knew. We thought it was set apart. My brother had just come up from there and he always looked at it good. He had been taking care of the stone, the furnace down there. We looked out the door and it was all glazed. We had some pretty treacherous neighbors at that time. There was one man, especially with treacherous, his wife told us about him because he was mean to her. Very mean to her. She was a good woman. She had relatives out here at Troy but he was mean to her and sort of cruel. Of course, she had a hard feeling toward him. He ran off with another woman. She came up to our house and she told us all the trouble. She told us all about what he was like. That's the way we got to find out about him. She said he belonged to the Black Hand Group. People did that. I said, well, it's just a dirty bunch. It was among some of the Swedes and different people that moved in here. I didn't know of anyone else but Roy. His wife told us that he belonged to that Black Hand bunch.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did they do?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: They just get in for people and they'll just do mean things just on purpose. Not that they have a grudge but just because they might not be the same nationality for that matter. No, they're just naturally mean. I don't know much about it but I know it's a very bad group. One of the very worst.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it part of that Swede in Norwegian?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: It's a Swede. He was Swede. He was an ... Swede. He could speak a broken English. His wife was much more intelligent than he was or very informed. She was a good woman. She was a very good woman. I think he made life quite miserable for her. She wouldn't have told us. She was so broken down about his running off with this other woman, leaving her there. I think he was gone a month or so.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He came back?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He had a pickup. Yes, he finally came back. The woman came back to her house when they lived on a little joining place. So they left this Hammond. That was his name. Her name was Hammond. They left our neighborhood then. Of course, they couldn't go there after an episode like that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: This was the Swede?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, I think the Hammonds were Norwegian on one side. I don't think they were on both sides. I just don't know them well. I didn't know them well. But they had two children. Very nice children. They attended school with us. She was just a kind of a... I don't know. She lived in Troy. Troy was an awfully wild town.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yes. The early day it was an awfully wild town.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why is that?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Well, so often in these little western towns, a lot of people would move into them. It just seemed like all of them had some very tough characters. Were there any... You don't have them those days and nowadays. Our towns are so bleak compared to what they used to be. We don't have that sort of thing.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there any characters in particular that you remember?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, I wouldn't like to mention them. Well, you wouldn't have to mention them by name.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Just things that people did. Well, I just never dwelled on anything like that. I used to hear it all. But I was one never to repeat things much. That was one of the rules in our home life. That they could discuss things with us at home that we weren't. To discuss with neighbors. You know, it makes trouble in the neighborhood. You can just... People can pack things and just keep the neighborhood all in uproar. But no. Mother was... They didn't even talk about your neighbors at all. My mother and father were exceptional people. My mother had very good breathing time. She lived in Tennessee. And she had... She was a... They were Scottish. They called them Scotch -Irish, but they were Spanish people. They were very strict in reading out their children. And teaching them the Bible and that sort of thing. And they believed in education. The families, as far as they could, could be educated in those days, were educated. My mother's sister married a doctor. That was in the neighborhood there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Which doctor was that?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: His... Dr. Newt Bailey was my mother's uncle. Her sister married Dr. Newt Bailey there in Tennessee. And he lived in Greenville, Tennessee. And his practice was in the surrounding country. And in those days, they'd go horseback. They'd go to the country. My mother said she thought so much of Dr. Bailey because when he had to come out to the country, he always came to their house because they were Scottish, you know. And eat dinner with them because he liked her mother's cooking. And her mother was a wonderful cook, I guess. I heard them say that she was. And some often that she would... I heard her mention things that her mother cooked. And we all of a sudden, my mother was a wonderful cook. Most people that came there always were pleased with the food that she cooked. She said, oh, she couldn't cook like her own mother. She could cook as good as her own mother did. So, maybe that's what she thought. But everybody thought mother was a good cook.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember the town of Troy being started?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Yes.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yes, it was. It was just a... We used to go to... We had some... There was a sawmill out in the flat on the other side of Troy. That meadow out there. I don't think they call it that. Meadow out on the other side of Troy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Now, west of Troy or north of Troy?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yes, it's west. Little north, north -western Troy. There's a meadow out there. And we had some... You see what the first...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You mean about where the ball field is now? Somewhere in there?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: It was there out in the woods, up the valley, up the valley there. Oh. Yes, up the valley there. And there's a creek king down there. I forget the names of that creek. That's a large creek that comes down. It comes out the north of Troy and comes down there. I think that creek still comes down through there. Anyway, there was a sawmill out there. And these people... There was a people that worked at a sawmill. They were good friends of my father and mother. There was always... Usually in those days, there was a little... The church usually held them together. There was a little Baptist church on Burnt Ridge. And we all attended church from Government Road and Burnt Ridge to Burnt Ridge. We attended church out there at Sunday School. And they didn't have preachers to go around everywhere. Finally. Finally, it was different. But in the early days, I don't know. When I was a youngster, just... About three to four years old, I don't know if I was any older than that or not. No, I wasn't any older than that. We drove there to Sunday School. Father was super -gen to the Sunday School and other people from Government Ridge. We drove out there to Sunday School on Burnt Ridge. It was just a little log house. Log cabin. There was a side of the road. I think the highway goes up just about where the cabin used to be. I can find it out. Some people by the name of Martin's. They're just across the road up on the hill above there.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: And there's one... I think there's still in Moscow a man that they've been working with most of that same family.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I'm sure he does.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He lives out in the heart of Moscow. I guess they could tell you more than I could because they had lived on Burnt Ridge and raised their families. This man grew up there. He's just a boy by the name of.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did the town of Troy sort of get itself going? What was it like before there was a town there?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: You see, there was a man by the name of Palmer. He was the big banker in Lewiston.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you know him?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, my folks did. They knew him very well. He was a... I think he was Jewish. I don't know. He came from New York to Lewiston. He built a big saloon there. A big mercantile house. He made most of his money to begin with. They say that he started on a barrel of whiskey. A barrel of whiskey and he divided that blood barrel up into several. Now that saves... Right. But that's what they told us. That's the story they tell about him. Yes, that's the story they tell about him. He got his start from selling a barrel of whiskey there in Troy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: In Troy or in Lewiston?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: In Lewiston, yes. In Palmer, they tried awful hard to get something named for him in Lewiston. He was a very grasping man. He didn't come there to build a place up. He came there to make money himself and he did. He made money over hand or fist. He put up a store, a mercantile store there. People would come there and run in and rob bills. Finally, they couldn't pay their bills and he picked a man. He owned a potlatch ridge and a round jettison over here. That's when he got into people's farms. Now, that wasn't good business. That was good business for him, to get money for him. But to build up a country, that is not the way to do it. If you're going to build up a country, you have to build up the farmers. The farmers, instead of getting everything they have, then he'd rent those farms to people that weren't usually especially good farmers. The land got all run down, all around Lewiston. Over here at Genesee, that country was just a weed patch. Because he didn't get the best farmers on there, just people. Well, he got the farm from and that's about all he wanted. People were familiar. He owned all that land. So, Bober didn't have a very good day when he came to Troy. Then he started that name.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He started the Respect for Troy. They didn't call it the Respect for Troy then. I don't know what. I think they called it the Bonner then. I'm sure it was called the Bonner then.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You can inquire some of those people.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there a little town there?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: It was a little village. People were built all over the hillsides there. They had a church. I think the Lutherans had the main church there. I think there was a Methodist church at one time.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: So, people got down on him because he started him at Troy. Just like he did around Genesee and Lewiston. Taking people in debt to him and then taking the property. That's no good banker. No good banker will do that. I'll tell you how. Just to tell you now, you don't need to use this for a note. Just to contrast the difference between a banker that large and a banker that will build.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Martin Thomas moved into kindergarten. He lived on American Ridge. He made real good on American Ridge. He took over the bank. It was one of the first banks of Kendrick. I forget what they called it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The farmers?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, it was before the farmers. It was the one down there about where Frank Barton's post office is. There's about where they first paid close to Troy and Kendrick. Martin Thomas began to help the farmers. He found them, give them advice and tell them what they could do and what they couldn't do. We had farmers that would just buy anything they could buy and give it their own time. Well, Thomas didn't believe in them. He saw that they paid for what they got. That's what built up people around him. Yes, absolutely. The bank and that bank and Kendrick have been like that ever since. They try to see if a farmer is not doing the right thing. They give them advice that they should have and tell them what to do or to quit. They're ready to take the property. No, I don't think Martin Thomas would have taken the man's property. He just started a new trend. I think Grandbok, he's fallen the same around her wife. Just the same because he worked. He started to get under Martin Thomas there, Kendrick. I don't think he liked Martin Thomas very well because he was very good at rachi. He wasn't very stylish. He wore good clothes, but he didn't think very much about clothes. He was just no farmer there. He didn't have a running though. He had a good financial mind because my husband was the director of the bank there for over 20 years.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He never did have anything to say about Martin Thomas. The only thing I heard him say about Martin Thomas that he didn't like. He said he put too much confidence in some people. People that he wouldn't have confidence in. As long as King was there, he always thought about my husband. He would advise Thomas about people. He would take his learning for it because my husband was very good at judging people. An exception. I doubt if they've ever had a man in Canada for just being able to judge people and still get along with them and not make them angry as my husband was. I know him. King died in the middle of the write up of the paper. It pleased me because they said that King Ingle was the favorite of this entire area. He had a good name.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He was the most friends. I don't know just how it was written. I had it someplace. I could tell. But anyway, they gave him a very good reputation for building and giving love to people. He lost the building. I know that after he passed away, several of the young men came and this community came to me. I told them, he says, you know, King, it was just a father to me. There was a lot of wildness in the country at that time. And the drinking and the leading boys astray. And King was just against that. He had been well brought up. He'd tell them where they go, what had happened to them. If they kept on. One young man, he was an ingle here, I can tell you this. I didn't want you to put it in the writing. He told King, you know, they were great to get together, great.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: And they offered King something. King said, oh no, he didn't think he wanted any. Well, this young man said, he said, if you don't drink, you can't associate with us. This is what King wanted, so he didn't want any of it. He said, you won't have any friends in this neighborhood. Well, he wanted against King, so he didn't care for this. But he finally got tired of it, I guess, and left. He owned this place over here. And he went to California. He finally died, you know, and just kept on his old, bull -headed ways, I guess. He was there in my life. Someone would not ever change, they'd get something in their head. They thought that if you were going to be a man, you had to drink beer and liquor with them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So, but they didn't get King, did they?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: King was just as strange as he could be. All he might take a sip of whiskey or brandy, you know, just a bunch of milk to drink. He didn't care for those things. He hadn't been brought up that way. He wasn't afraid. He'd get started drinking drink because he wouldn't do that. But then most of them would, you know, they'd get started and then they never quit. It sort of ruins them in a way, I think. Ruins their character. Because they get associated with that element of people. And makes life just a little bit harder for them than if they had gone their own way. Most of them. You find out and you just can find a better man than any of the people that had lived here in New England. Would tell them that he was my husband. I've never seen anything that could ever take his place with me. I knew just how fine he was. How good and how interested he was in people. It wasn't that he wanted to criticize people, but he didn't want to see them ruin themselves. By taking up things that they'd make habits of that they probably would never hurt people. Clean to them during the money. Well, that's kind of a man my husband is. So, we had a happy life together. He was a good worker and a good planner and a big business animal.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He was a good man. He could do something he wanted to do.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Could you tell me a bit more about the early days of Troy?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Well, I didn't live there. So, someone that lived there could tell you so much more.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How about those early days around Little Bear?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: We were about 20 -oh, Little Bear Ridge. I could because I lived in Little Bear Ridge. I knew all the people on Little Bear Ridge.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Some of our first settlers there were Swedish people. They were very good Swedish people though. I mean, there weren't enough of them that they were Plenish. We built a church on Little Bear Ridge. The Methodist Church went up there. Those people always attended church at our church, at the Methodist Church. That was the only church on Little Bear Ridge. That's what I liked about the Swedish. They were not Plenish because the church they had grown up with. Well, they didn't refuse to come to our Methodist Church on Little Bear Ridge. I respected them for that. There were Scandinavian churches up north in this north country. North of Little Bear Ridge. Back up there. They used to attend church up there later quite a few years ago. But they weren't built. They had churches in Troy, of course, first on Little Bear Ridge. Our church was built earlier.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did people get in general along pretty well out there?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yes. There were always a few cantankerous ones in the neighborhood. That was South Asian wanted to run things and be bossy. But people kind of ignored it. There wasn't much quarreling.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I suppose I could think back enough. I could recall some crafts. But I didn't follow that line very much.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did people cooperate a lot when they worked?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I think they're very cooperating, yes. They help each other in our debate. That's the only way. They had to. They got along.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: They didn't have hired men as a rule. The father used to hire some Swedish people up in the country. We had a man by the name of Erickson that worked for us. He'd come and run in the summertime. He was a good fan, a very good fan. I think when he first came down on the farm there, he'd never ridden horses.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: My brother Charlie, who was always full of fun, he told him, he said, how do you get on him?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Well, he got on with his head toward the tail.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Finally, Charlie laughed at it and told him how to ride. That's the way he got on the horse when he first got off. Facing the tail.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: They didn't know about guiding the horse or anything about it. He was just a new one. I don't think Charlie would tell anybody about facing the tail. I don't think he would. I wouldn't say he didn't. When I was not there, I didn't hear it. Charlie was full of tricks. He used to like to talk to him and learn English from him. He liked to teach some English. That's the one thing Charlie got the best at. He was teaching English to the Scandinavians that came there that couldn't speak English. They liked to do it. He said he even helped only women speak English. He taught him. He worked with him. He taught some of his first English. That was my oldest brother.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you attend school there on the little marriage?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I attended until I was... That was the only school I attended until I went down to Kennedy. We would just go there year after year. We didn't have very long turns. We would sell to get through a book that we started in during the school year. Because the school years weren't over three months or something like that. Then we would have to go two or three years to get a year of school. Just short terms.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I had gotten so I just knew the elementary subjects by far. That is what we had in our country schools. Elementary subjects like grammar and physiology.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: The arithmetic was one of the main ones. That was on my... I liked arithmetic. I could think better than anything else. I liked grammar too. I don't know how many grammars I went through before I even went down to high school. When I got tired going to the country school I said I'm not going to this country school anymore. Now my father said the older girls are the normal. You go down to the normal. They had a preparatory at the normal. Two years preparatory and two years professional. They would do the high school work in two years. In preparatory. He sent my older sisters to Lewiston to go to the normal. That is what they took. They hadn't had high school. So they had to have preparatory work. I told them I was going to go to the school. So I got on the horse and I started in. We always had ponies to ride. I started in. I was riding through the rain down there. One of the women told me, she said, I'd like to have you just to come and stay with me. I found my little boy and he'd go out and he'd just come with you. Your board will be free if you just come and stay with us. So I did. I stayed with them.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: After several months I finished up staying in another place.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: They were sitting with their children. My little boy. I don't know if he was in school but he liked play checkers. That was the best approach he liked to beat all the time. So I would play. I would see that he won. He won several games. Finally I won. Oh, he gets so mad. He's just furious. He thought he was winning. I was just letting him move so that he could dream again. He said it was fair effort. I was telling his sister.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: His folks have this meat business out of Troy. Not at Troy but in Moscow.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, Davis?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, not Davis. Up on the hill. Up on the hill there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, what are the names?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Everybody knows. They've been there for years and years. I don't know about their ads in the paper. But anyway,

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I was telling him, oh yes, I let him read. Then he gets so furious that I let him win. I'm telling his sister about it. She was down there at Kennedy one day. We ate dinner together.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I said, how is Clarence getting along?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He was the oldest one in the family. She was pretty younger than Clarence. I think Clarence was nine years old when he was born.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I told her then about this playing checkers movie. How he wanted to win all the time. How many he didn't win. He didn't win. She said, he still wants to win.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I know Clarence would be one that wouldn't change. He was just that way. That stood out in that one trait of character. He wants to win. If he didn't win, he mustn't please. If his mother had taught him now to take it in, well, to return and not get angry when he won, but be pleased. Be glad to see someone else win part of the time. No, he wasn't that way. He wanted everything. He didn't win.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did you live on Little Bear Ridge with your folks?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I lived there until I was married in 1999. From that time on, I lived over here. I was married at my home there on Little Bear Ridge on June 23, 1999.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Your father died there on Little Bear Ridge, didn't he?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yes, because of his death. The horses ran far away down the road.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Wait, what horses are these?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: The team that he was driving. The team that he was driving ran far a short distance down the road. Then they started up that steep hillside to the left. That would be on the same side that the lottery trial was, but it was farther on down the hill. Up that steep hillside, ran way up there. They turned around and came back down the hill. See, there was a big, steep bank there. If you've been up on Little Bear Ridge Green, you know how it's like there, remember where that screen was.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: The wagon hit the road and overturned there, and threw farther off on the rock pile, just on the edge of the road.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He was not speechless. He never did get his speech back.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He doctored for many years, and then he lived, see, did I say two years or three years?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I think I did it at that moment. Two years, I think. I think it was two years that he lived.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: He never did get his speech back, but it injured the nerves of his throat and chest. He got bubbly, perhaps what they call bovaer paralysis. That is a disease of the nerves, and a set of nerves dies each time. If you look it up in a medical book, you see it's a slow, it doesn't come out once, but they just start him dying, these sets of nerves, until it, I don't know, that's the moment. So that's, he lived two years with that bovaer paralysis. That's the way it acts. That's what the doctors told us, that bovaer paralysis acted. Two sets, one set dies at a time, until the last set of nerves. It would be the one that would control your heart. So he lived two years, and he was, all of that time though, Father would get a pen, pencil, and his pen cycles, and he would write in here. He would tell if I needed to say anything you wanted to tell us, or ask you so you can try. So his mind was, it didn't affect his mind at all.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: It was just the nerves of his spine, and then they'd go.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was Kendrick a pretty good little town most of the time?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Pretty prosperous little town.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Or was it as wild as Troy?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Oh no.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, I think they had their wild ones there.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I don't know how wild Charlie is though. I've never lived there. It's not wild anymore. No, I didn't think it was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I think they have some pretty good churches there, haven't they? And people can search pretty well, don't they?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I think Troy is a pretty fine little town now. It's improved a great deal over a lot of laws in those days.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: They did make discrimination there, I think, though, in people that are, I think that people who are intelligent and trying to build up Troy, they respected them directly. I think Frank Green was respected here at the Illinois tribe. That is, Frank brought his father in law, his wife's. I knew his wife. I used to go to school with Frank Green's wife. Her name was Rhonda Herbert. The Herbert's weren't thought very much of, but Rhonda was just always a wonderful person. The French wife. I knew her as a girl. She was a very sweet person and very, very fine. I used to walk to school with her, always home from school, as far as I came down from home. I always write, write, and I was very fond of her. I never knew anything like her before. She was just a fine person. Frank Green married her. She was a very attractive little girl. She was young for him.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: But then, I guess, didn't she die when her baby left?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: It seems to me like she did. He was quite a few years older than she was. They lived and farmed on Little Berries, where my folks farmed.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there, was there, you were saying about, earlier about, cattle gains, cattle rustlers that...

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Well, there were cattle rustlers, but we lost quite a bit of cattle. But we never traced them down.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They never knew who they were?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: No, never knew.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I never, we never found out. We had a special because of the cattle gain. There was a ring, and it was from here to Colfax, and Juliaetta was mixed up in that cattle business too, a great deal.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think the ring was centered in Juliaetta?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I've been told it was, yes. There were some of the very worst ones in Juliaetta. Juliaetta used to have a pretty tough man. Just from, well, they had a winery there, and they made beer too. They distilled a little beer there too.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I guess they distilled beer, don't they?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: They grew it. They grew it, they grew beer, that's right. Well, they had a, oh yes, a beer brewery there too. So it was well supplied with liquor, and Henry had three saloons. I knew women in Tenderloin. They'd cross the street before they'd go in front of the saloon and dodge the saloon. They wouldn't even pass in front of the saloon. Women were that straight back in the middle of the state, a lot of them. I wasn't so afraid of going past the saloon. I kind of would have to look at the pile of beer kegs that were standing out in front, and not just count them and see how many they were potting up. I was younger though. Gosh, you guys love accounting things.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: One story I heard about this road out here, the road that goes down the grave of the big barrier, was it the people on Potlatch Ridge who wanted a new road, and the governor came up here, and the people from Bear Ridge sort of snatched the governor away and took him on a ride up this road here?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I never heard of it.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I think that's all they did. Okay, I didn't know. I don't think, no, I'll tell you what happened. Down there in Boise,

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: there was a boy that had grown up in Boise and lived there. Martin Thomas was in the back down there, and this is inside information that I never told anybody. He wrote up this boy that had lived there in Kendrick, and he was very well thought of. He wanted to know what we thought of this road up Bear Ridge. He said, now people say they're building it up there just to favor King Angel. Well, King wrote back and told them if they put a road across the big Bear Ridge, they couldn't go across the big Bear Ridge without crossing Ingleland, because our land spreads out from the far side over there, way down over in Ingle. You see, clear down here to that mooring place. The Ingleland spread. So that's what I told them. Some people would say King Angel wanted it just for himself, and he tried to lay it on King, and King was in the bank because he was a director of the bank down there. So he got the inside information. Now this is inside information. That's what he told the boy, and this boy could advise the person who was the chief man interested in building the highway up there, and he told him. But King made it. So they made that on the Martin Harris River. I think the story I heard wasn't about this highway, but it was about the older one yet. Oh, the older one yet. No, I don't know about that, the older one, because that was just built by the county, I think, in those days. I think the people in the county built those all -woods, early roads. They took their terms of working on the roads, and they built the roads and didn't get anything out of it. That was just, these grades, the first grades we had made were built almost entirely by, well, they would have a road supervisor, and he might have been paid something. But I don't think they got any money out of it. Early days, I know my father, who had built lots of roads, and that's the way they did it. They built the roads themselves. They'd get someone to sign a road up for where it ought to be, and then they'd just go ahead and have their plows and diggers and shovels and things like that, and go ahead and make the road. I know that road up there, it's the first time I went over there. It was just wide enough for one of those big black ones to just make your kids feel almost, no, bags are steep down below there. Mother couldn't stand it. She'd get out and walk behind the wagon, because it was so steep. We had come from California where we had little roads. And these little sites, it's more of a steep place. We had to use the... ...soul level there. We had a wonderful place there. Perhaps it was too bad we ever left. But still, it was interesting there. It was very interesting. I never regretted it for so long.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: It sounds like when you came here, you got into a much more pioneering sort of thing.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yeah, that was, yes. We got into a mixed... We didn't have pioneering much down there. California had been all of it's civil. It was quite civilized at that time. We had some very fine neighbors down there. And our schools. Father was a director of the school. And they took a great deal of interest in their schools. At that time, when we came from California, we were supposed to have one of the best school systems in the United States. Because they had taken so much interest in their schools and building them up and having good teachers and right -hand teachers. No, that's what we missed when we came to Idaho. They had no schools here at all. We just had to, the students had to build up after we came here. Just a little old log cabin there on the bare edge. And they used it for church and school too. Now that was... I just went to school there one year. No, I guess I didn't. I visited the school. My brothers and sisters had visited the school. But I didn't start to go to school until I was seven. Then we had a schoolhouse. When I was seven years old, they built a schoolhouse just across the road from the Nelson place. Over on the south base there. The place south of the road. To see who lives there. Now, all...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Across the Cheery Nelson place?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yes, yes. Just south. Just a road runs between the places. Yes, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Is it a Nelson place?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, it didn't. Benjamin is on the north side. He was on the south side. Yes, you know. There's a Norwegian family that lives there. And one of them married one of the emmett girls of the boys. He owns the old farm place.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I'm not sure. Ryerson. Oh, Ryerson. Ryerson, yes.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Ryerson's. I think they're Norwegians. And his wife, Ryerson, the Nelson place there, married an emmett girl.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: One of the Emmets from Genesee?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, that don't live there at all in the Norwegians. Ryerson.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: You see a girl along down here. Is, is, is her home is up there on the little bridge. Not just north of there. My, my, she's a little jealous all the time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, your place was the place?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: South of, I don't know whether Benjamin's live on it there or, or, I don't, I don't. Emmett's own, own the emmett land. But they rented, they rented the one.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where was your place from Cherry Nelson?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Oh, Cherry, it was, it was farther over to the, to the south and to the east. We were on the lower end of the ridge.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Could you, could you see the Cherry Nelson place from your place?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Oh no, no. Well, I guess we could if we get up on a hill. Yes, we could I think. But it wasn't very visible to us. It was a, they had them besides trees up there, lots of pine trees there. That was the surrounding area. We were over on the edge of the canyon, our home was. And that's where our home was. We had land farther in on the ridge. Our home was right on the canyon, around the canyon. We could do Big Bear Ridge better than we could Little Bear Ridge or the North End of Little Bear Ridge. No, there was a, we had a highway up to Cherry Nelson place. It wasn't Cherry Nelson place, it was Downcorder place in the early days there. Lorenzo Downcorder was on that place. And then he sold to my brother -in -law Oscar Hogan. And Oscar Hogan sold to Cherry Nelson.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, I don't know of anything else.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: With all the, all the land you had, you must have hired a lot of, a lot of labor, didn't you?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, we didn't have a lot of farm land.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So much of our is land in the canyon. I mean after you and King were married. Oh, after we, no, we, we always had labor.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: We always had to have some of the farm land. I was wondering if you. We would have a hired van.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you ever have the IWWs come in when you were?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, we were never founded. King didn't have any use for them. No, we didn't have any trouble.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They wanted to control labor, didn't they?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Because it wasn't that, what the IWWs were.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They were the, they call themselves One Big Union.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Oh, yes. And, and, and. No, the IWWs never bothered us much. We usually had a man, we'd get people. So often in those days we'd get Swedish people from the north that come down to farm. They loved to learn to farm and they would do what you tell them. And most of our hired helped. They were the hardest hired help for Swedish people. They worked for year after year. Then we'd probably go farther up north, up to that northern side up there. And, uh, Hawkins, Hawkins used to come down there and plow for father in the spring. Mandolina Hawkins, they were some of the earliest settlers.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you come over on Potlach or Princeton?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: No, no, it was, uh, I'll take all that up there for.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Farmington?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: No.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: I don't have a map, I'll get the shortcut to you. Avon, uh, north Avon, north Avon.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: But, uh, there's some people around that, you see them, that down here in Juliaetta that used to live up there. They grew up up there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About when do you think the towns that were, the Juliaetta and Troy were picking, about when do you think they started to sort of tame down a little bit?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Well, they, the railroad helped a great deal. Getting the railroad through so people could haul their grain to the local markets and shipping. Anything like that helps your town if any, uh, any produce from the farms that sold in the town helps to, help build it. So I think, I think the warehouse business helped. We had some awful, terrible warehouse people. People. But, uh, we had some good ones too.

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: So, they, uh, I think that helped to build, and our school, school helped to build the church. The church helped to build Kendrick. We had a good Methodist church at Kendrick. We had some very good ministers.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember the Kendrick fire?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Oh yes. Yes, I wasn't, they, uh, there's two or three fires there. One of the biggest fires was in about 1904, I think. That was one of the, that was the year I graduated from high school there at Kendrick. But, uh, I was at home on the little bear ridge and I could see down there and see that, see the fire burning. The doctor, there was a doctor at our home at that time. And, uh, my sister was there. Her youngest boy had a calorie and phantom. He was doctoring. And, uh, that fire burned the drug store in Kendrick. And, uh, they, uh, he had to, and he lost nearly everything in his office too because that was right in the, uh, joining the drug store. So, he had to move to, to Juliaetta. And, uh, they, they moved his practice down there to the drug store in Juliaetta. Well, this little nephew of mine that had calorie and phantom was, uh, doctoring by Rothwell. They moved his medicine down there and they, they, they sent the prescription down to Juliaetta and had it filled. And they filled it. Doctor Rothwell wasn't in the drug store, you know, but they, the drug just filled the medicine. It filled the, filled the prescription. And, uh, brought it up there and that little boy began to get worse and they called Doctor Rothwell again. And he came up and he says, let me see what, what that medicine was. And he looked, took the bottle and he looked at it and he cursed. Oh, Mother said he just walked up the floor and cursed. He was, he was a little rather profane man. He was a very smart man, but he was, but that was the way he had to let it off his feet. And he said, that child can't live. He'll die. And he did. He died in the stairs.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He was in the wrong medicine?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Yeah, I'm in the same.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it just a mistake?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Just, they didn't know, you see. See, the, uh, the druggist down there didn't feel the crime. He was in the wrong medicine.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they have anything to do with what happened to the druggist for doing that?

speaker: Florence Liney Ingle
words: Well, we did.
